questions.q: What is the security risk of deserializing untrusted pickle data, and what is the correct policy?
questions.a: pickle.loads() executes arbitrary Python bytecode embedded in the data. An attacker who can supply the pickle payload can run any code with the process's privileges. The policy: only pickle data you generated yourself; never unpickle data from external sources, user input, or network responses.

questions.q: Map each of these use cases to the correct format: external API response, developer tool config, internal cache of a trained model.
questions.a: External API response → JSON (human-readable, language-agnostic, safe to deserialize). Developer tool config → TOML (readable, typed, standard for pyproject.toml). Internal trusted cache → pickle (only acceptable because the data source is controlled and trusted).

questions.q: When is CSV preferred over JSON for external data, and what is its main limitation?
questions.a: CSV is preferred for tabular data (rows of uniform columns) sent to or from spreadsheet tools or data pipelines. Its limitation is no native type system — all values are strings, so numbers, booleans, and nulls need explicit parsing.
